notes: Round 9 research focused on MD, DE, WV, FL. Cross-referenced against fights.json (727 entries) and all previous new-fights-march2026 round files (rounds 1-8). Found 18 genuinely new entries (7 MD, 5 DE, 2 WV, 4 FL) plus 14 status updates for existing entries (7 MD, 2 DE, 3 WV, 2 FL). After this round, total entries per state: MD=16, DE=8, WV=8, FL=8. Key findings: Frederick County CDI-OZ referendum exceeded 18,000 signatures ahead of March 20 deadline; Calvert County initiated 24-month moratorium process over AWS/Lusby; NCC DE passed comprehensive regulations 12-0; DE SB 205 tabled in committee; Tucker County WV air permit appeal filed in court March 6; Berkeley County WV $4B Penzance project announced; FL SB 484 passed legislature (weakened) awaiting DeSantis signature; St. Lucie developer withdrew Sentinel Grove application; Project Tango postponed to April 23.

opposition_groups: Frederick County Data Center Referendum Committee
new_entries.summary: The Frederick County Data Center Referendum Committee collected over 18,000 signatures (exceeding the 15,000 required) by the March 20 deadline to put the CDI-OZ overlay zone to a ballot referendum. The 2,614-acre Critical Digital Infrastructure Overlay Zone was approved by the County Council on Dec. 23, 2025. Organizer Steve Black expects a legal challenge from the county or the committee regardless of the Board of Elections' decision. Backed by 13+ nonprofits including the Farm Bureau and Nature Forward.

Calvert County Commissioners voted unanimously on March 3 to initiate a possible 24-month moratorium on data center approvals after AWS job postings for data center construction in Lusby surfaced on LinkedIn. An AWS representative told commissioners 'nothing is in place' for a project. The vote launches ordinance drafting and public hearings; the moratorium hearing is expected in April 2026. County already updated zoning in March 2025 limiting data center locations.

St. Mary's County is facing data center development pressure as part of the broader Southern Maryland trend alongside Calvert and Charles counties. Local advocacy group Informed St. Mary's has been tracking data center regulation issues. The county is monitoring developments in neighboring Calvert (AWS/Lusby) and Charles counties as developers eye the region's available power infrastructure near the Pax River military complex.

new_entries.project_name: HB 270 Data Center Impact Analysis and Report
new_entries.summary: Maryland General Assembly overrode Governor Wes Moore's veto of HB 270 (Data Center Impact Analysis and Report) during a special session on Dec. 16, 2025. The law requires a comprehensive study examining how large-scale data centers affect electricity demand, water resources, infrastructure costs, and local economies. This provides the baseline study that multiple proposed moratorium bills (including HB 120) reference as a prerequisite for resumed permitting.

Del. Jesse Pippy (R-District 4) asked Frederick County's state delegation to consider a bill placing a moratorium on new data centers in the county until the statewide HB 270 impact study is completed. This is separate from the statewide HB 120 moratorium bill. The bill would specifically target Frederick County data center development pending the results of the mandated statewide impact analysis.

Delaware SB 205 would require large commercial energy consumers (data centers using 100+ MW, amended up from 30 MW) to obtain operating certificates from state regulators, with mandates to pay for grid modernization or commit to additional energy generation. Sponsored by Sen. Stephanie Hansen. The bill failed to advance from the Senate Energy Committee in early March and remains tabled while Hansen works through business community concerns. Companion HB 233 (separate rate for large energy users) was voted out of House committee Jan. 28.

new_entries.project_name: HB 233 Data Center Utility Rate Separation
new_entries.summary: Delaware HB 233 would require PSC-regulated utilities like Delmarva Power to establish a separate rate class for large energy-use facilities (20+ MW primarily engaged in web hosting, streaming, or data processing). Sponsored by Rep. Frank Burns and Sen. Stephanie Hansen. Voted out of House committee on Jan. 28 with 5 favorable and 5 'on its merits' votes. Awaiting full House floor vote. Ensures data centers pay fair share and don't shift infrastructure costs to residential ratepayers.

new_entries.project_name: St. Georges Business Park Data Center
new_entries.summary: Developers of the St. Georges Business Park in Middletown are exploring conversion to a data center campus, which would require demolition of Frightland, a popular Halloween attraction in Delaware. This is one of three separate projects in Newark, New Castle, and Middletown where developers have been quietly exploring options to turn office parks or land into server hubs. Public hearings on data center regulations are scheduled.

Verdantas submitted plans to build a 676,000 sq ft industrial park at White Clay Center Drive east of Newark, proposing to demolish existing office and industrial buildings (some formerly JPMorganChase offices) for data center conversion. This is one of three Northern Delaware sites exploring data center development, alongside projects in New Castle and Middletown.

Tucker United, WV Highlands Conservancy, and Sierra Club filed a Notice of Appeal on March 6 with the WV Intermediate Court of Appeals and WV Supreme Court, challenging the Feb. 5 Air Quality Board decision upholding the Ridgeline data center's air permit. The groups contend WVDEP improperly classified the 500-acre natural gas power plant/data center as a 'synthetic minor source' rather than a 'major source,' limiting public oversight. They also challenge redactions in the permit application that conceal emissions data.

opposition_groups: League of Women Voters of West Virginia
new_entries.summary: The WV House (88-12) and Senate (28-6) approved new rules for Department of Commerce data center and microgrid certification under HB 2014. Despite 930+ public comments raising concerns about environmental, economic, and local control impacts, legislators voted down amendments for water protections and local control provisions. The law strips local governments of authority to regulate certified data centers, exempting them from municipal zoning, permitting, noise, and lighting regulations. An amended version returned to the House for final approval.

The developer behind the $13.5 billion Sentinel Grove Technology Park withdrew their application after Governor DeSantis introduced legislation giving local governments more power over data centers. Commissioner Cathy Townsend confirmed the developer 'pulled out because they knew legislation was coming.' The proposed 1,200-acre, 15 million sq ft facility would have been the largest data center in Florida. The withdrawal may be temporary; the developer is waiting to see final legislation before deciding next steps.

A proposed 7+ million sq ft data center on 434 acres at Yeehaw Junction (US 441 north of FL Turnpike/SR 60 interchange) faces opposition due to its proximity to the 27,000-acre DeLuca Preserve, crucial habitat for the endangered Florida grasshopper sparrow. Plans call for nine three-story data center buildings of 675,000 sq ft each. Developer 'US Development' is seeking to rezone from agricultural to industrial. Environmental groups cite threats to one of the state's most important wildlife corridors.

Indiantown Village Council voted unanimously Feb. 26 to approve a land-use change for 5,722 acres owned by Florida Power & Light, paving the way for Silver Fox 606 LLC's proposed 2.2 million sq ft data center (five two-story buildings). The first of three required public hearings was expected in March. Developers claim 400 jobs. Construction would occur in three phases. The project faces scrutiny as part of the broader statewide debate over data center impacts.

new_entries.project_name: Hester Data Center Operating Certificate Bill
new_entries.summary: Sen. Katie Fry Hester (D-Howard and Montgomery) introduced a bill requiring all data centers of a certain size to receive a certificate to operate from the PSC. The bill would create a load-joining queue requiring data centers to apply for a certificate of operation before drawing energy from the grid. Utilities would analyze transmission impacts, expected electricity use, energy generation plans, and economic importance. Described by Hester as 'basically a transparency bill, so that we'll have everything in one place at the state.'

Six organizations from five Maryland counties formed the Maryland Data Center Analysis Group (MDCAG) to assist local groups statewide with data center policy, provide verified information on impacts, and offer balanced policy guidance. Members include Envision Frederick County, Sugarloaf Alliance, Fellowship of Scientists and Engineers, Montgomery Countryside Alliance, Sustainable Hyattsville, and the Tri-County Coalition. Operates alongside the 40+ member Maryland Data Center Reform Coalition focused on General Assembly action.

Deltona Corp. filed a request for a comprehensive plan amendment covering roughly 813 acres in northern Citrus County to allow for industrial uses including data centers, though no specific project has been proposed yet. The rezoning request is part of the broader wave of data center interest in central Florida. Tech giants including Amazon and Meta are eyeing the region as Florida becomes a new hotspot for AI infrastructure.

new_entries.project_name: New Castle County Data Center Ordinance
new_entries.summary: New Castle County Council voted 12-0 (1 absent) on March 11 to approve comprehensive data center regulations after 7 months of debate. Key provisions: 1,000 ft buffer from residential zones (500 ft with site-specific noise study), ban on open-loop cooling (unless using reclaimed water), mandatory energy-efficient generators, and site restoration requirements. The regulations do NOT apply to projects already in the pipeline, including the controversial Project Washington. The Delaware Building Trades Council has been running candidates for county council seats in opposition to the regulations.

updates.summary: UPDATE: The CDI-OZ overlay zone (2,614 acres) enacted Dec. 23 now faces a citizen referendum. The Frederick County Data Center Referendum Committee collected 18,000+ signatures (15,000 needed) by the March 20 deadline. Organizer Steve Black expects a legal challenge from either side regardless of the Board of Elections' outcome. If signatures are validated, the CDI-OZ goes to a special election ballot this fall.

UPDATE: HB 120 was assigned to the Environment and Transportation Committee and had a hearing in early February. As of mid-March 2026, state-level moratorium bills in Maryland (like many other states) have stalled in committee, while local moratoriums have gained traction. The bill remains pending but has not advanced to a committee vote. The broader trend shows local success outpacing state-level moratorium efforts.

UPDATE: Opposition to the Brightseat Tech Park (former Landover Mall site) intensified in early 2026. The proposed $5B, 4.1 million sq ft, 820 MW data center campus by Lerner Enterprises faces environmental justice concerns in the predominantly Black community. County Executive Aisha Braveboy's September 2025 permitting pause remains in effect. Activists cite environmental racism concerns.

UPDATE: County Council voted unanimously Feb. 2 to pause data center permitting through end of 2026 and require a planning board study by Oct. 1. County Executive Elrich proposed additional restrictions: air pollution and noise limits on diesel generators, 80% self-supplied energy, and construction limited to industrial zones. However, Elrich said Dickerson data center developers 'have a right to go forward' and would not be subject to the moratorium.

UPDATE: Baltimore County Council unanimously passed Councilman Pat Young's moratorium bill, blocking all data center building permits until Jan. 1, 2027. No applications for permits will be accepted. The Planning Board is directed to review existing laws and regulations and recommend updates. The moratorium was triggered by a proposed 42-acre, 150 MW data center in Woodlawn.

UPDATE: Charles County continues advancing zoning text amendment ZTA 25-187, which was briefed to the County Council on Oct. 7, 2025 and forwarded to the Planning Commission. Charles County is now part of the Southern Maryland data center pressure zone alongside Calvert and St. Mary's counties as developers eye the region.

UPDATE: New Castle County Council voted 12-0 on March 11 to approve comprehensive data center regulations after 7 months of debate. Key provisions: 1,000 ft buffer from residential zones (500 ft with noise study), ban on open-loop cooling (unless using reclaimed water), mandatory energy-efficient generators, and site restoration requirements. The regulations do NOT apply retroactively to projects already in the pipeline (including Project Washington). Separately, the Delaware Building Trades Council is running candidates for county council seats in a dispute over the regulations.

UPDATE: Starwood Digital Ventures appealed DNREC's Coastal Zone Act denial on Feb. 25. The appeal hearing is scheduled for March 24, 2026. DNREC Secretary Greg Patterson called the proposed 6 million sq ft facility 'unprecedented' -- the largest current coastal zone user has 8 generators; Project Washington would require 516 diesel backup generators. The 1.2 GW facility would consume more electricity than all Delaware homes combined.

UPDATE: After the WV Air Quality Board upheld the Ridgeline data center's air permit on Feb. 5, Tucker United, WV Highlands Conservancy, and Sierra Club filed a Notice of Appeal on March 6 with the WV Intermediate Court of Appeals and Supreme Court. The appeal challenges the 'synthetic minor source' classification and redacted emissions data. The Ridgeline facility is a proposed 500-acre natural gas power plant/data center between Davis and Thomas, developed by Fundamental Data. No court date has been set.

UPDATE: Governor Morrisey signed paperwork on Feb. 26 designating the Penzance Management $4B Bedington Campus as WV's first 'High Impact Intelligence Center.' The 548-acre campus in Falling Waters would deliver 1.9 million sq ft and 600 MW of IT capacity. Del. Chris Anders (R-Berkeley) criticized the announcement, warning about power and water impacts. The Morrisey administration and Penzance have refused to provide basic project details. No state funding was used; entirely private $4B investment.

UPDATE: The WV legislature approved new certification rules under HB 2014, voting down amendments for local control and water protections despite 930+ public comments. The House passed 88-12 and Senate 28-6. The law strips local governments of authority over certified data centers. Conservation WV, WV Highlands Conservancy, and League of Women Voters of WV have been leading the opposition. An amended version returned to the House for final approval in the last week of the legislative session.

UPDATE: Project Tango's hearing has been postponed to April 23, 2026, pending additional impact studies. The project has been scaled back from 3.69 million sq ft to 'only' 1 million sq ft. A Sun-Sentinel editorial demanded lifting the secrecy around the project. The developer (Palm Beach Aggregates/Phillips Inc.) remains unnamed as to the end user. A Change.org petition has nearly 4,000 signatures. NDA secrecy remains a flashpoint as SB 484 would allow state agencies to keep developer plans secret for a year.

UPDATE: SB 484 passed both chambers and now awaits Governor DeSantis' signature. The final version is significantly weakened from DeSantis' original proposal -- the Florida House inserted provisions allowing state agencies to sign NDAs with developers for up to a year after conversations with the pro-AI White House. Key remaining provisions: data center companies must pay for their own utilities (not ratepayers); local governments retain land development authority. Effective date would be July 1, 2026 upon signing.

UPDATE: Carroll County's moratorium is part of the broader wave of Maryland county-level actions. At least five Maryland counties (Baltimore, Montgomery, Carroll, Prince George's, and now Calvert) have enacted or are pursuing moratoriums or permitting pauses on data centers as of March 2026.
